text: <par>For a similar final correspondence, consider WT <latinform>ŋed</latinform> 'I' and Jinghpo <latinform>ŋèmdash ai</latinform> 'I'.</par>

<par>This form attests to the simultaneous presence of the <reconstruction>*s-</reconstruction> and <reconstruction>*m-</reconstruction> prefixes with this root: <reconstruction>*s-m-lay</reconstruction>.</par>

<par>It is not clear what phonetic feature Luce was attempting to transcribe with his symbol <<latinform>s'</latinform>> (e.g. in Hakha, Hwalngau, Khualsim, Tiddim, and Zotung); a Hakha consultant in Berkeley pronounces this word with what sounds like an ordinary [<latinform>s</latinform>]. In general, PTB <reconstruction>*s-</reconstruction> > Proto-Chin <reconstruction>*t(h)-</reconstruction>, while PTB <reconstruction>*ts-</reconstruction> > Proto-Chin <reconstruction>*s-</reconstruction>.</par>
<par>Luce gives another Zotung form <latinform>ʃɯ¹</latinform> for WOMB that I include under <xref ref="1613">#1613</xref>.</par>
<par>Note the denasalization before a voiceless stop of Womatu <latinform>yak¹</latinform> (< <xref ref="1283">#1283</xref>).</par>
<par>For the first syllable, see <xref ref="1283">#1283</xref>, below.</par>

<par>Note the similarity between the Mru forms for 'tail' and the Monpa forms <latinform>ɕampi</latinform> (42159), <latinform>ɕam-pʰi</latinform> (39413), and <latinform>ɕam⁵⁵ pi⁵⁵</latinform> (39234); the 'p' in the Monpa forms may be epenthetic.</par>

<par>Note the similarity between the Mru forms for 'tail' and the Monpa forms <latinform>ɕampi</latinform> (42159), <latinform>ɕam-pʰi</latinform> (39413), and <latinform>ɕam⁵⁵ pi⁵⁵</latinform> (39234); the 'p' in the Monpa forms may be epenthetic.</par>
<par>For a similar final correspondence, consider WT <latinform>ŋed</latinform> 'I' and Jinghpo <latinform>ŋèmdash ai</latinform> 'I'.</par>
<par>For the last syllable of the Ganan and Kadu forms, see <xref ref="300">#300</xref>, above.</par>

<par>These Ganan and Kadu forms superficially resemble Loloish reflexes of <xref ref="596">#596</xref>, but they have been assigned to the present etymon because of their resemblance to other Asakian forms.</par>
